title: French Baguettes
subtitle: Mostly waiting, mostly worth it.
excerpt: French Baguettes. A home-cook's recipe from Rebecca's collection, kept in the family rotation.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook. Bread recipes draw on the long tradition of home baking. Elizabeth David, English Bread and Yeast Cookery (1977), is one of the canonical references in English.

content.text: A home baguette: thin, crisp-crusted white bread with a chewy, open crumb. The recipe makes three loaves about 35 cm long. Most of the time is resting and proving; the hands-on work is under thirty minutes spread across the day.

Two things matter most. The dough goes into the fridge overnight, which develops flavour slowly. The oven bakes with steam, which keeps the surface elastic long enough for the loaf to spring open at the scores before the crust sets.
What you need
Method
Mix all ingredients in a bowl. Cover with cling film and rest for 15 minutes.
Over 90 minutes, do 3 sets of stretch and folds, turning the dough upside down after each set.
Cover the bowl with cling film and refrigerate overnight, 12 to 14 hours.
Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface. Divide into 3 equal pieces and shape into rough rectangles. Cover and rest for 45 to 60 minutes.
Place a baking stone or heavy baking tray in the upper half of the oven and a deep roasting tin on the bottom rack. Start heating the oven to 260°C (240°C fan). Boil a kettle.
Stretch each rectangle slightly and fold into a cylinder, sealing the seams. Roll gently to stretch each loaf to about 35 cm.
Place on a lightly floured cloth or board, seam side up. Cover and prove at room temperature for 30 to 60 minutes until clearly puffed.
Transfer the baguettes to baking paper, seam side down. Dust off excess flour. Using a very sharp knife or a bread scorer, make 3 diagonal cuts along each baguette with a swift, firm motion.
Pour the boiled water into the hot roasting tin to create steam. Slide the baguettes onto the baking stone. Reduce the oven to 245°C (225°C fan). Bake for 15 minutes.
Remove the water tin, rotate the baguettes, and reduce to 230°C (210°C fan). Bake for a further 15 minutes until deep golden brown. Cool on a wire rack before cutting.

attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.fix: Check yeast in warm water with a pinch of sugar. It should froth in five minutes. Prove in a warm spot (airing cupboard or oven with the light on).
items.cause: Yeast was stale, or the dough was too cold.
items.symptom: Bread is dense and didn't rise

items.fix: Preheat to 220°C with a tray on the bottom. Pour in boiled water when the loaf goes in. Steam gives crust.
items.cause: Oven not hot enough, or no steam.

items.fix: Give it longer, until the dough doesn't spring back when poked. Shape gently so the bubbles survive.
items.cause: Underproved, or the dough was knocked back too hard.

items.fix: Bake on a heavier tray on the middle shelf. Lining with baking paper helps.
items.cause: Tray was too thin, or rack too low.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong

content.text: Variations
Seeded crust: brush the top with water and roll in mixed seeds before the final prove.
Wholemeal: replace 100 g of the flour with wholemeal for a denser, nuttier loaf.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers
Best fresh on the day. Day-old baguettes work well toasted or used for croutons. Freeze baked loaves once cooled; reheat from frozen in a hot oven for 8 minutes.
Where this dish lives

The baguette is the everyday bread of France, eaten at breakfast with butter and jam and carried under the arm from the boulangerie. The home version is slower and more rewarding than the bakery loaf: the overnight cold prove builds the flavour that a fast commercial process cannot.
